Well, let me explain (for simplicity, every time I say a `Pipeline` it could be that or a `ColumnTransformer` or a `FeatureUnion`...): 
* We need to have the parameter `input_features` because otherwise, when the Encoder is part of a `Pipeline`, there will be an `Exception: BaseEncoder.get_feature_names_out() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given`, because the method will be called with the parameter `input_features`. 
* Now, what can we do with this parameter? There were two options: modifying all encoders to take into account how to generate the output features given the input_features, or just using the `feature_names_out_`, which is available and computed. Given that this library internally work with dataframes, I thought that the second option was a much better idea. There is only one caveat, which happens again when the encoder is part of a `Pipeline`: if the previous transformer outputted a numpy array, then the column names won't be good (they will be like col_0_A, col_0_B...). That's why I think we should inform that, to ensure full compatibility and 100% correctness of feature names, `sklearn` should be used with the `set_output="pandas"` option. In this case, everything works smoothly, as the tests prove. 
* Another way to put it, in `sklearn` it makes sense to have a function with this signature, because an estimator can be fitted with a numpy array and then it should be able to "compute" the feature names of the output given a list of input_features. But for `category_encoders` this use case does not have too much sense, as the input is expected to be a pandas dataframe. And this assumption is spread everywhere: even other parameters work with column names, like the `mapping` or the `cols`; you won't see parameters expecting column names in `sklearn` because they expect that the input can be a numpy array. 
* So, yes, I agree the behavior is different than in `sklearn`, but I think it makes sense as long as the library is based on this "pandas assumption". I think a big redesign will be a good idea but as we discussed in the issue it will take a lot of effort.

No, and it seems I didn't make it clear enough in the comment. `step == 0` can only happen in two cases:

1. The image only has a single value, meaning there are no pixels that don't have the maximum value. No real image will ever hit this  since the image contains no information whatsoever.
2. The image has multiple values, but the number of non-maximum valued pixels is lower than 255. I think this is an unintended side effect of the PIL algorithm. In a real use case, I think we should also never hit such an image. It either has to be really small like 16 x 16 or we have another unrealistic distribution of values where most of the pixels have one high value.

There's no way to create scalars with TFRecords. If your features represent scalars (as is usually the case), you still have to use list like `FloatList`.

Let's say you're working with the Iris dataset. Your TFRecords would look like this:

```
feature={
            "sepal.height": tf.train.Feature(float_list=tf.train.FloatList(value=[3.5])),
            "sepal.width": tf.train.Feature(float_list=tf.train.FloatList(value=[3.2])),
            "species": tf.train.Feature(bytes_list=tf.train.BytesList(value=[b"iris"])),
        }
```

If we didn't special case, your dataset that looks like:

```
Dataset(..., schema={"sepal.height": object, "sepal.width": object, "species": object}
```

This makes it impossible to use preprocessors and difficult to write UDFs.

What you probably want (which is what you get with the special casing) is a dataset like this:

```
Dataset(..., schema={"sepal.height": float, "sepal.width": float, "species": object}
```

I understand that special casing is generally bad, but based on the examples I've seen online, it seems like single-elements list represent scalars in the vast majority of cases. I've never seen a use case where a single-element list actually represents a list.

If we remove the special casing, `read_tfrecords` might not work the way users expect it to.

I'm not sure this is right. Here's my logic for you to audit. When python sees
```
x += y
```
it translates that to
```
x = x.__iadd__(y)
```
(if x has an `__iadd__` method). In this case `__iadd__` doesn't return anything so while the original object x referred to gets mutated, the variable x ends up with the value `None`.

I think the simplest way to implement `__iadd__` for a JsonArray would be to emulate the update-in-place behavior of `list.__iadd__` and go with 

```
def __iadd__(self, b):
    self.extend(b)
    return self
```

I mean, at the end of the day it's just my own personal API preference, so it deserves a heaping mound of salt. But I think my preference for an atomic ``my_run_process(...)`` over an async context manager, is that the context manager introduces a second concurrency mechanism to trio -- it's behaving both like a subprocess spawner, and a nursery, and my personal preference would be to keep those two separate. It was also my experience that the context manager resulted in more boilerplate, but I think that's the weaker point so I'm not sure it's worth exploring very far.

Like I said though, I'm just one person, and I always reserve the right to be wrong haha. Just thought I'd add some thoughts into the mix!

> Why do we need to flush every time a new node is picked? Isn't it fine to just pick a different node if the GCS service restarts?

We must ensure that every change of the node needs to be flushed. Consider such a scenario. If we choose Node1 to create Actor1 at the beginning, and the resource of Node1 is not enough and spill back a Node2. Then the GcsActorScheduler will try to create Actor1 at Node2. Suddenly then GCS crash and restart, it will load the data of the previous flushed to the storage. If the change is not written to the storage before creating Actor1 on Node2, the GcsActorScheduler will create Actor1 on Node1, which will cause the Actor1 be created on both Node1 and Node2.

1) Some of the zopefoundation repos have a custom `Extension` subclass that attempts to detect compiler errors and print a warning if compiling an extension fails (zope.interface does that). This logic is required when parsing tables for which text is not spaced or aligned consistently within a cell.  For example, consider a complex periodic table such as [this](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4d/Periodic_table_large.svg). The atomic number is listed in the upper right corner of a cell of the table, the standard atomic weight in the top left. Using the current logic, the layout parser will group  the atomic number of one element and atomic weight of another element (across a dividing line). This new logic will ensure that all the values within a cell are grouped together and respect the boundaries drawn by lines.  Thus, a given text box would contain only the information for one element.

The trick is to never do `cache.set('mykey', MARKER)`. 

The core of the idea is this:

```python
marker = object()
value = cache.get('key', marker)
if value is marker:
    # the key was not in the cache
    value = compute_value()
    cache.set('key', value)
return value
```

If `cache` was a pure Python dict you wouldn't need this because you could do:
```python
if 'key' in cache_dict:
    value = cache_dict['key']
else:
    value = compute_value()
    cache_dict['key'] = value
return value
```

So that's, potentially, **two read queries** from the data structure. One to ask "Do you even have this key?" and one to ask "Alright, what's the value of this key?". If the network latency matters, it's oftentimes more performant to send only **1 read query** and deal with consequences if it comes back empty. 

But, even with a pure Python dict, it's sometimes pleasant to use the `.get()` method because you only need *1* branch:

```python
marker = object()
value = cache_dict.get('key', marker)
if value is marker:
    # the key was not in the cache dict
    value = compute_value()
    cache_dict['key'] = value
return value
```

In the last example, suppose you didn't use the "marker techique"...


```python
value = cache_dict.get('key')
if value is None:
    # the key was PROBABLY not in the cache dict
    value = compute_value()
    cache_dict['key'] = value
return value
```

...then, what if `compute_value()` genuinely returns None. What *would* happen is that the caching would not protect `compute_value()`. And that's what you want to achieve.

Regarding malicious behavior on the leader's part pertaining to dropping transactions or not committing during their tenure, we would at least want the following to be true:

* If a leader commits _at all_ during their tenure, then they commit at least one transaction on-chain that the next leader can build off of.
* An honest leader should be able to recover from periods of inactivity without paying a penalty, but we only want leaders to be able to be able to do so if there were no on-chain commitments.

We can guarantee these if the leader must commit their Stacks block's coinbase transaction to the burn-chain.  To see why, consider these four possible outcomes when a leader L begins its tenure:

1. L commits to a microblock from the previous leader L_prev, and announces their microblocks in a timely fashion (the happy path).
2. L does not commit to a microblock during their tenure.
3. L commits to a valid microblock, but does not announce their microblocks.
4. L commits to a range of one or more leaderless blocks (i.e. the case where L_prev did not commit to a block during its tenure), and may or may not announce its microblocks.

In case 1, everything works out like we'd expect.

In case 2, L cannot broadcast anything. All nodes will ignore L's microblocks, and L will not receive a coinbase reward (i.e. this is equivalent to L opting to burn their coinbase). No other previous or future leaders' rewards are affected.  This discourages L from doing nothing, and L gains nothing by withholding transactions (see below).  L_next, the next leader, can recover in case 4.

In case 3, we need to make it so L has until the end of its tenure to broadcast microblocks.  If it doesn't, then the system needs to orphan any "late" microblocks -- in particular, L shouldn't be able to reorganize the chain by sending them late (otherwise they could do shenanigans like selfish mining).  To do this, we can make it so that if the next leader L_next doesn't see any of L's microblocks, L_next commits to L's (on-chain) coinbase microblock as the chain tip. In doing so, L_next forfeits any transaction fees L might have passed on to them, but at least L can't make any ill-gotten gains since its unannounced microblocks are now orphaned.

In case 4, L needs to restart the chain.  That is, L_prev (and possibly previous leaders before it) was in case 2 during its tenures -- it didn't commit, and cannot send any microblocks.  To recover, L must (1) commit to the last active leader's last-seen microblock (which could be its coinbase), and (2) indicate how many previous epochs since then had inactive leaders. L's commitment is only valid if it indicates the exact number of previous inactive leaders; otherwise L's commitment is invalid. If L mines off of a microblock from the last non-empty leader, then L does not have to pay a fork penalty of any kind (discussed later in this STIP). L may mine off of a microblock from an even older non-empty leader, but pays the fork penalty for doing so (discounting inactive epochs).  If L successfully commits, then if L broadcasts its microblocks, we're back to case 1. If L does not broadcast its microblocks, we're back to case 3.

`to_consensus_bytes()` returns a serialization of the _consensus-critical_ fields of the node. In practice, this is everything but the `ptr` value of its `TriePtrs`, since the location of the nodes on disk (or in RAM) doesn't need to be agreed upon by nodes in order to correctly handle queries and inserts.  However, the node types, paths, parent/child relationships, and hashes are consensus-critical, since they are what define the structure of the Trie.

That said, `to_consensus_bytes()` only serializes the node's internal data, not its hash or its sibling hashes.  This is important, because the choice of which hash to use is dependent on the MARF structure, not the Trie structure -- i.e. the hash of a child node that lives in a different Trie is the hash of the _block_ in which it resides, as opposed to the hash of the node's data.  This fact is why the hash is _not_ stored within the `TrieNode` implementation.

The fact that a child is in another Trie is captured by `to_consensus_bytes()` already -- the highest bit (`0x80`) of the child's `id` in its `TriePtr` will be set if so, and will be cleared if not.

Ah, so I think I see the error.  Or at least one part of the error.  So network objects are supposed to be copyable in the usual way, e.g. `net1 = net2`.  This is however broken here in `compute_loss_multiclass_log_per_pixel` since its constructor does `work = device_global_buffer();` which puts a device specific buffer in the classe's state.  But when you go and copy a network to another network, as happens incidentally during deserialization when the trainer reloads all the networks, you run into the problem of all the networks using buffers for device 0.  

I think the right solution is to move the `work = device_global_buffer();` from the constructor to inside `operator()`.  There isn't anything particularly expensive about calling `device_global_buffer()`.  The only reason to save `work` (and `buf` really as well) is to ensure they have lifetimes as long as the cuda kernel launch that happens there.

Hi all, cluster_environments is a list of environments that were found via clustering, meaning that each environment in cluster_environments corresponds to the environment of a single "cluster" of particles with similar environments (ie neighborhoods). The way the code works is that cluster_environments[i]  is actually the average environment taken over all the environments of particles in cluster i. So if cluster 0 consists of 4 particles (indexed k1-k4) which have similar environments defined by their 6 nearest neighbors, then cluster_environments[0] is a set of 6 vectors, where each vector is the average taken over the corresponding matching vector in the environments of particles k1-k4. As an example, cluster_environments[0][0] will be a vector, calculated as the following average: (k1_environment[0]+k2_environment[0]+k3_environment[0]+k4_environment[0])/4. Each vector in every particle's environment is defined _with respect to the particle_, which is what enables these averages to take place across different particle environments. Put another way, one could imagine placing each particle at the origin when defining the vectors that make up its environment. So I would say "cluster_environments[i] contains the average environment of the cluster of particles that is indexed by i. Each vector in cluster_environments[i] is defined with respect to an imaginary particle sitting at the origin." Does this make sense and seem okay? Feel free to change the wording, especially of the word "imaginary." But I can't think of a clearer way of saying it- presumably the user will understand how environments are generally defined throughout the code, and so saying that this variable contains "average environments" won't be too confusing?

The averaging process can be seen in the code, if you are curious or if looking at the code itself would be clearer than my explanation: line 626-631 of [environment.pyx](https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/blob/master/freud/environment.pyx) shows that cluster_environments returns the set of environments produced by getClusterEnvironments() in [MatchEnv.h](https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/blob/master/cpp/environment/MatchEnv.h) (defined lines 283-286). That function just returns the object m_cluster_environments, which is populated in lines 656-660 of [MatchEnv.cc](https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/blob/master/cpp/environment/MatchEnv.cc) from the object cluster_env, which is computed in lines 632-639 of MatchEnv.cc via the method getAvgEnv (defined lines 192-238). Wow now that I actually summarized it I'm not sure if this is clearer than my explanation... but just in case you want to see how it is working under the hood, that is what's happening!

I think it's great to clarify this section, but I don't think pybind11 does anything actively, which means that "pybind11 will always ensure" isn't accurate.

It's just that there is a general (but not enforced) contract that everyone is meant to adhere to: any thread calling any Python C API function must hold the GIL.

Maybe:

When Python calls into C++ via pybind11, and assuming the process is healthy, the current thread is guaranteed to hold the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) and pybind11 will never implicitly release it:

```
new code block here
```

The classes :class:`gil_scoped_release` and :class:`gil_scoped_acquire` can be
used to acquire and release the global interpreter lock in the body of a C++
function call. In this way, long-running C++ code can be parallelized using
multiple Python threads, **but great care must be taken** when any :class:`gil_scoped_release` appear: if there is any way that the called C++ code can call back into Python, via callbacks, or trampolines, `gil_scoped_acquire` will be needed.

For callbacks based on `std::function` (pybind11/functional.h), pybind11 will implicitly manage the GIL, most importantly when a Python callback is passed to
C++ code via ``std::function``, and the C++ code calls the function, the GIL is acquired before the Python callback is invoked. Similarly, the `PYBIND11_OVERRIDE` family of macros will acquire the GIL before calling back into Python.

Well, the reason I don't like this is that there are too many things it will catch.  I like catching exceptions and giving friendlier messages, but specifying which one would be more useful.  For example, you could have:
```
try:
    mod = user_modules[self.platform][self.name]
    inst = importlib.import_module(mod)
except KeyError as kerr:
    estr = "unknown platform or name supplied to user modules: {:} or {:} not in {:}".format(self.platform, self.name, user_modules.__repr__())
    logger.error(estr)
    raise KeyError(kerr)
except ImportError as ierr:
    estr = "unable to locate or import module for platform {:}, name {:}".format(self.platform, self.name)
    logger.error(estr)
    raise ImportError(ierr)
```

Then, if something else breaks we'll still get it in the intended form and can fix or catch it as appropriate.

So the issue here is that the code takes an arbitrary task from the task group to see what projectId it has. If scopes are sufficient for _that_ task, then the transaction is approved for _all_ tasks in that task group, even if the client that calls this endpoint doesn't have the scopes to delete all the tasks. This should be changed to return the unique projectIds, and require `queue:cancel-task-in-project:<project>` for _all_ projectIds that exist in the set of tasks that it would like to cancel. This is one of the reasons I am wary of implementing this directly in the database, and not gating it on the `cancelTask` implementation (which would ensure consistent scope treatment).

This is why, in this PR, I chose to default `User-Agent` to:

```python
f'wikidata-based-bot/{__version__} (https://github.com/dahlia/wikidata) python/{python_version}'
```

unless specified otherwise by the end-user, i.e.:

```python
    def __init__(self,
                # [...]
                 user_agent: Optional[str] = None) -> None:
        # [...]
        if not user_agent:
            python_version = f'{sys.version_info.major}.{sys.version_info.minor}.{sys.version_info.micro}'
            user_agent = f'wikidata-based-bot/{__version__} (https://github.com/dahlia/wikidata) python/{python_version}'
        # See also: https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/User-Agent_policy
        opener.addheaders = {
            'User-Agent': user_agent,
        }
```

This way, by default, a compliant `User-Agent` is always compliant with Wikidata's policy by default.

> A Python distribution contains a `Makefile` and a `pyconfig.h` header file that are necessary to build both the Python binary itself and third-party C extensions compiled using [`distutils`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/distutils.html#module-distutils).
> 
> [`sysconfig`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sysconfig.html#module-sysconfig) puts all variables found in these files in a dictionary that can be accessed using [`get_config_vars()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sysconfig.html#sysconfig.get_config_vars) or [`get_config_var()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sysconfig.html#sysconfig.get_config_var).

[`sysconfig.get_path()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sysconfig.html#sysconfig.get_path) is something I've used to get the module path for example:
```python
>>> sysconfig.get_path('purelib')
'/usr/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages'

'/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages'
```

> Not saying this was my favourite route; but it would still work, the restriction being that the node runnable be a `system_binary(name="node")`.

This would work, but it could become cumbersome (it would need more docs

> turning to dedicated fields for specific use cases that becomes very target specific where I would much prefer a more general solution to lean on 

Agreed that we'd prefer a more general solution. #18320 suggests that the best way to tackle this would be through scoped dependencies (what you call "annotating the connection", which is effectively what's going on with `_runnable`).

`adhoc_tool` highlights that there's two different axes for dependency scoping: _where_ the dependency is used (e.g. a dependency of the output of the tool vs. a dependency needed to produce the output) and _how_ the dependency is used (e.g. as data for the tool at runtime vs. as an executable the tool can execute). 

We've (temporarily) solved the "where" question by having two separate fields for dependencies, but the downside to this is that we can't make runnable dependencies transitive, because we can only declare runnable dependencies where this field is defined. The result is that build files end up being repetitive (see [the example I've been working through](https://gist.github.com/chrisjrn/42677a04f6631779d2e0002d0acca624)): you need to define and redefine runnable dependencies. Better would be able to describe -- precisely once -- that `yarn` needs a command named `node` on its `PATH` when run, and then anything that needs to depend on running `yarn` will have that underlying `node` binary available. That's not possible without the cooperation of core/plugin authors if we require new fields to describe each context for dependencies.

I'd like to see this achieved with the standard `dependencies` field, so that things like `execution_dependencies` can disappear entirely. In the meantime, experimenting within the `execution_dependencies` field feels like a good way to see if the idea works? 

---

Re. using a custom object vs dicts or strings, I leaned towards custom objects because it allows for automatically validating the metadata for the dependency (in this case, that a `runnable` dependency needs a name for the binary). I am open to other ways of doing this.

I think it's debatable. From that page:

> What should I do if I update my own dependencies without changing the public API?
>
> That would be considered compatible since it does not affect the public API. Software that explicitly depends on the same dependencies as your package should have their own dependency specifications and the author will notice any conflicts. Determining whether the change is a patch level or minor level modification depends on whether you updated your dependencies in order to fix a bug or introduce new functionality. I would usually expect additional code for the latter instance, in which case it’s obviously a minor level increment.

In my experience, some Python projects bump the major version when dropping support for a version of Python and some do not. We have never done it ourselves in the past and no one ever complained about it. We were not planning on updating the major version now and I still don't think we should.

I think it causes more harm than good since it will cause downstream packagers/users to worry about API changes when they're aren't any. If someone tries to install Certbot on Python 2 (with semi-updated versions of `setuptools`/`pip`), it will either install the last version that worked with Python 2 or refuse to install it saying Python 2 isn't supported. Us bumping the major version does not help them in my opinion.

My brief reading of this PR leads me to believe that there's a sharp edge:  a user could still hit a segfault if they call `py::cast<mismatched_holder_type>(my_object)`. Totes get the idea of wanting to shift overhead from `py::cast<>()` (runtime) to function declarations (binding-time, e.g. `.def()`), but it now means there are now distinct entry points to this type-checking that users can trigger :(

Possible resolutions:

- if it's worth the risk, then just mention it in the holder documentation (as a diff in this PR)
- If it's not worth the risk, somehow enable raw `py::cast<>()` to do a runtime check. This may make for some crazy plumbing.
- Alternatively, eat the cost of runtime casts and funnel it through `type_caster<>`. That's what we do for our fork ([`RobotLocomotion/pybind11`](https://github.com/RobotLocomotion/pybind11/blob/58a368ea8e89638e01a7385cad9ce4345d70003d/README_DRAKE.md)), and we haven't yet had to point fingers at performance there (though our use case may be simpler).

@YannickJadoul or @rgwk Any chance y'all have a good (and mebbe easy?) timing performance benchmark for this, to see what the risk is in these terms?

```python
def as_float(value):
```

```
class GenericInstrument(FakeInstrument):
    #  Use truncated_range as this easily lets us test for the range boundaries
    fake_ctrl = Instrument.control(
        "", "%d", "docs",
        validator=truncated_range,
        values=(1, 10),
        dynamic=True,
    )
    fake_setting = Instrument.setting(
        "%d", "docs",
        validator=truncated_range,
        values=(1, 10),
        dynamic=True,
    )
    fake_measurement = Instrument.measurement(
        "", "docs",
        values={'X': 1, 'Y': 2, 'Z': 3},
        map_values=True,
        dynamic=True,
    )


class ExtendedInstrument(GenericInstrument):
    # Keep values unchanged, just derive another instrument, e.g. to add more properties
    pass


class StrictExtendedInstrument(ExtendedInstrument):
    # Use strict instead of truncated range validator
    fake_ctrl_validator = strict_range
    fake_setting_validator = strict_range


class NewRangeInstrument(GenericInstrument):
    # Choose different properties' values, like you would for another device model
    fake_ctrl_values = (10, 20)
    fake_setting_values = (10, 20)
    fake_measurement_values = {'X': 4, 'Y': 5, 'Z': 6}


def test_dynamic_property_unchanged_by_inheritance():
    generic = GenericInstrument()
    extended = ExtendedInstrument()

    generic.fake_ctrl = 50
    assert generic.fake_ctrl == 10
    extended.fake_ctrl = 50
    assert extended.fake_ctrl == 10

    generic.fake_setting = 50
    assert generic.read() == '10'
    extended.fake_setting = 50
    assert extended.read() == '10'

    generic.read()
    generic.write('1')
    assert generic.fake_measurement == 'X'
    extended.read()
    extended.write('1')
    assert extended.fake_measurement == 'X'


def test_dynamic_property_strict_raises():
    strict = StrictExtendedInstrument()

    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        strict.fake_ctrl = 50
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        strict.fake_setting = 50


def test_dynamic_property_values_update_in_subclass():
    newrange = NewRangeInstrument()

    newrange.fake_ctrl = 50
    assert newrange.fake_ctrl == 20

    newrange.fake_setting = 50
    assert newrange.read() == '20'

    newrange.read()
    newrange.write('4')
    assert newrange.fake_measurement == 'X'


def test_dynamic_property_values_update_in_instance():
    generic = GenericInstrument()

    generic.fake_ctrl_values = (0, 33)
    generic.fake_ctrl = 50
    assert generic.fake_ctrl == 33

    generic.fake_setting_values = (0, 33)
    generic.fake_setting = 50
    assert generic.read() == '33'

    generic.fake_measurement_values = {'X': 7}
    generic.read()
    generic.write('7')
    assert generic.fake_measurement == 'X'


def test_dynamic_property_values_update_in_one_instance_leaves_other_unchanged():
    generic1 = GenericInstrument()
    generic2 = GenericInstrument()

    generic1.fake_ctrl_values = (0, 33)
    generic1.fake_ctrl = 50
    generic2.fake_ctrl = 50
    assert generic1.fake_ctrl == 33
    assert generic2.fake_ctrl == 10


def test_dynamic_property_reading_special_attributes_forbidden():
    generic = GenericInstrument()
    with pytest.raises(AttributeError):
```

@nealjack thanks for all the changes. Some small naming suggestions below:

In OT, core is c++, but all public APIs as defined in C.

Since this definition is restricted to core (c++ source), we don't need the `typedef` anymore (`enum Type` already defines a type named `Type`).

Also the `ot{Name}` convention is typically used for public C OpenThread definitions. Within core modules, we don't use the `ot` prefix (since everything is already under `namespace ot`).

For constants, the `OT_{NAME}` is typically used for public C OT definitions. Within core (c++) the convention is `k{NameInCamelCase`}`.


So suggest the following:
```
enum BlockType  
{
    kBlockType1 = 1,
    kBlockType2 = 2,
};
```
(this `enum` type is already under `class Coap` so it would get namespaced as `Coap::BlockType`).

Let's imagine a situation where someone is running a SQL database without constraint checking. It might be possible that `[Animal_2].parent IS NOT NULL` is satisfied but the join to `Animal_3` still doesn't find anything. For the not-null case, might we be better off checking that the primary key on `Animal_3` is non-null?

Put another way, the foreign key column being `null` guarantees there's no edge there. But it being non-null only guarantees there's an edge there if constraint-checking is on, and deletes run with `CASCADE` set. While it's a good idea to do this, many databases and systems do not.

If you think it's too much work, it's fine either way. But if it's about equal work either way, let's err on the side of supporting more use cases.

> #2 would include a default server to run with(say uvicorn) ootb but then you can also override it with maybe --asgi-server=module.path.to.runner.

The problem is there isn't a standard way to call an asgi server from code. 

* `uvicorn`:
```python
import uvicorn
uvicorn.run(app, host='0.0.0.0', port='0.0.0.0')
```
* `hypercorn`:
```python
# https://pgjones.gitlab.io/hypercorn/api_usage.html
from hypercorn.asyncio import serve
from hypercorn.config import Config

config = Config()
config.bind = ["localhost:8080"]  # As an example configuration setting
asyncio.run(serve(app, config))
```
* `daphne`:
```python
# Doesn't provide documentation on how to run the server from code
```

> I think I would at the very least like an ootb implementation where the serve command worked with a default supported/tested implementation.

I think we can provide an ootb implementation that works with `uvicorn` and `hypercorn`, and the user can select which server wants to run with `--asgi-server=hypercorn` (default to `uvicorn`). Something like:
```python
if arguments.asgi_server == "uvicorn":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host=argument.host, port=argument.port)
elif arguments.asgi_server == "hypercorn":
    from hypercorn.asyncio import serve
    from hypercorn.config import Config
    config = Config()
    config.bind = [f"{argument.host}:{argument.port}"]
    asyncio.run(serve(app, config))
else:
    raise Exception(f"Server {arguments.asgi_server} not supported")
```

On the one hand, we value correctness and having the same behavior between aiohttp and flask.
On the other hand, if we change the behavior in a way that isn't backwards compatible, then we should probably either be staging a major version bump, or at least understanding the breakage risk very well.

Quoting from https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/error/runtime_error

> The pointer is guaranteed to be valid at least until the exception object from which it is obtained is destroyed, or until a non-const member function (e.g. copy assignment operator) on the exception object is called.

From that I think a `const_cast` is compatible with expectations for `std::runtime_error`.

But the upcast idea has me very worried. If what I see depends on the order of the upcasts and `.what()` calls, it could lead to extremely confusing debugging. IOW I'm budgeting wasted human time on that vs the performance gains. The performance gains may never be "actually consequential", nice of course, but the hours lost in confusion, at scale almost a certainty, can really hurt. — I don't think this can be quantified precisely, but I sure know such pain!

I think it would be better to not make this overly smart/sneaky.

Idea: `std::runtime_error(nullptr)` to die if someone tries to use the unavailable`.what()`
Non-starter: `what():  basic_string::_S_construct null not valid`

Next idea (maybe even the better one): `std::runtime_error("This is an up-cast pybind11::error_already_set: details are unavailable. Please catch & inspect the pybind11::error_already_set object.")`

Never change it.

Confidant's dynamo usage is a bit weird. We use a single table for all record types, and we store history and current records in the same table. For performance, we have two types of record per model. So here, we have `service` and `service-archive` type records. The `service` record is the one that's queried, when `get_service` is called. When history is looked up, it's the archive records.

If we were to split the records into separate tables, we would use hash+key, which would let us drop the double-save. It's something I've considered, if we were to get rid of blind credentials (which we can do with proper RBAC).

Join is different from other ops because it's done rank-by-rank, at different times.
All ranks need to communicate if any other rank just sent Join request, because this may be the last joined rank and Join response will be sent by the communicator.
These other ranks will not have the Join request in their own queue, so the cache is used to let them know someone else just joined.

There is absolutely no harm in letting those classes be initializable, and everything that doesn't need to be in the code, but is, is increasing complexity.

For people not that familiar with python, seeing `__new__` in a class can be very confusing and seem quite complex, and sure it technically isn't that complex if you're familiar with it and know how it works, but there are countless of people who just don't know that, and they shouldn't need to go through the struggle of looking it up just to understand this code, if it isn't even all that necessary. I don't mind having a bit more complex code in places where it's necessary, but if it can be avoid it, it should be avoided.

Type checking is not the issue here, it was just a thought I brought up as something that could fill the position of the `build` method, instead of it just being a wrapper. It is true that this method isn't necessary there though, I only suggested it if preventing initialization was that big of a concern, since it's a simpler way to do it than to have a class like this.

However again, there is no harm in letting these classes be initializable, the user shouldn't really even need to be initializing the full status response classes, since they're generated by the status methods on server classes. If they decide to initialize them, they probably know why they did that. There is no good reason to prevent it completely.

> This class preventing initialization abstract classes, so in that way user can be sure that he got JavaStatusResponse or BedrockStatusResponse. Not an abstract class, without any real code.

I don't see how a user could be confused on whether they got an base class or a real one, if they initialize it themselves, they will know what class is it, and if they get it from a method like `status`, it will certainly be the full class, not a base class.

- If the spinel interface support hardware reset, the `WaitResponse(false))` is used for waiting for RESET reason response.
- If the `aResetRadio ` is set to false and the Spinel interface doesn't support the hardware reset.  The `WaitResponse(false))` is used for waiting for RESET reason. When the RCP starts, it sends RESET reason automatically to the host. The openthread simulation uses this case.

In your case, I assume that the  `aResetRadio ` is set to `true`. If failed to reset the RCP stack via the spinel commands, the `WaitResponse(false)` should time out and the host should die.

Unfortunately, identifying async functions is rather subtler than just `inspect.iscoroutinefunction`, because async functions can be wrapped in a way that doesn't satisfy that test. (For example, Trio functions decorated with `@enable_ki_protection` are implemented using a wrapper that looks synchronous. `functools.partial` objects don't even satisfy `inspect.isfunction`, let alone `inspect.iscoroutinefunction`. All of these are perfectly valid to pass as an "async function" as long as the function being wrapped is async.)

The best way to see if something is an async function is to call it without `await` and use `inspect.iscoroutine` on the result. Of course, if it wasn't an async function, you just executed it, so it's best to do this only in the context where you were planning to call it anyway. In this case, that would be inside `unprotected_afn` below.

If you're not willing to call the function, you have to get a lot more special-cased to see if it's async or not. You can look inside the `.func` attribute of a `functools.partial` object, follow the `.__wrapped__` attributes generally added by function wrappers, etc, but there's no approach that will definitely succeed for any async callable.

TL;DR: After offline discussion, we decided to replace `datetime.timedelta` with `hightime.timedelta` across the board in all usages of `timedelta` in all modules in nimi-python.

Pros:
- Code generator is simpler, no `if-else` cases
- No need to carefully curate metadata, with respect to which `timedelta` to use
- Consistency within and across nimi-python modules
- No churn if underlying driver runtime or a new device family starts supporting higher resolution for some property or method parameter

Cons:
- Currently, certain modules in nimi-python do not have a need for higher resolution provided by `hightime`. Users of those modules will be forced to install a third-party library - `hightime` as a dependency and use `hightime.timedelta` type.

Given that `datetime.datetime` and `datetime.timedelta` are base classes for the respective `hightime` variants, with duck-typing, users should not have to do anything different to use `hightime` types, other than importing from a different package.

Pros clearly outweigh the cons, thus the decision to move to `hightime.timedelta` wholesale.

The `existence` keyword argument you are referring to is passed on the initialization of the channel class, while the keyword arguments we are referring to in this comment are passed on to the class method `_discover_from_instrument`, which does not have an `existence` argument.

```python
         # via  `load_from_instrument`

```

The keyword arguments supplied to `_discover_from_instrument` are whatever arguments are needed to load the channels correctly.

I think the major reason for combined affine transformation being fast is because it really has lower computational complexity.

Assume we have $k$ affine transformations $T_i$ in $\mathbb R^2$, where $T_i$ can be represented by 3x3 matrixes.

The sequential transformation can be represented as 

```latex
y = T_k (T_{k-1} \dots T_1 (x))
```

and the time complexity is `O(k N)` where `N` is the cost of apply one transformation to image `x`.
N is linear to the size of x.

For the combined transformation

```latex
y = (T_k \circ \cdots \T_1) (x) 
```

the time complexity is `O(27(k - 1) + N) = O(N)` where 27 = 3^3 is the cost for combine two transformations.

Rather than copying in all this logic from `_load_credentials_from_file`, please separate it out into a helper function, e.g.:

```python
def _get_credentials_project_from_json(info, source):
    """Parse JSON credential info into credentials and project.

    Args:
        info (dict): JSON credentials from file / environment.
        source (str): label for source of credentials (filename,
            env var)
    Raises:
        google.auth.exceptions.DefaultCredentialsError: if the file is in the
            wrong format or is missing.
    """
    credential_type = info.get('type')

    if credential_type == _AUTHORIZED_USER_TYPE:
        from google.auth import _cloud_sdk

        try:
            credentials = _cloud_sdk.load_authorized_user_credentials(info)
        except ValueError as caught_exc:
            msg = 'Failed to load authorized user credentials from {}'.format(
                source)
            new_exc = exceptions.DefaultCredentialsError(msg, caught_exc)
            six.raise_from(new_exc, caught_exc)
        # Authorized user credentials do not contain the project ID.
        _warn_about_problematic_credentials(credentials)
        return credentials, None

    elif credential_type == _SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TYPE:
        from google.oauth2 import service_account

        try:
            credentials = (
                service_account.Credentials.from_service_account_info(info))
        except ValueError as caught_exc:
            msg = 'Failed to load service account credentials from {}'.format(
                source)
            new_exc = exceptions.DefaultCredentialsError(msg, caught_exc)
            six.raise_from(new_exc, caught_exc)
        return credentials, info.get('project_id')

    else:
        raise exceptions.DefaultCredentialsError(
            '{source} does not have a valid type. '
            'Type is {type}, expected one of {valid_types}.'.format(
                source=source, type=credential_type, valid_types=_VALID_TYPES))
```

The main issue I have — in principle — with changing this file like this is that (... if I recall correctly ...) many of the functions in this file are taken verbatim from python standard library. The upshot of that is that if these functions are changed for some reason in future versions of python, it should be more straightforward to update them, as the amount of changes necessary besides copy/pasting is minimal. That's why I made `ForwardRef = ForwardRefWrapper` below — this allows the functions here to be that much less modified from their original sources.

On the other hand, I will  admit that this diff isn't exactly large. The biggest issue to me is that it might be easy for whoever ends up working on it (which might not be you, me, or Samuel) to not realize "oh I need to replace `ForwardRef` with `_make_forward_ref` everywhere.

One solution, perhaps, is to add an explicit note about this at the top of this file. Or maybe it's just reasonable to not say anything and we just deal with it in the future if/when necessary. Anyway, if @samuelcolvin is good with this then I am.

I do recall us discussing whether to call `removeEventListener()` in the context of a browser event listener.  iirc, the concern was that browsers would not clean up properly and cause extra memory to be retained.  I don't think this is the case, but I wasn't able to prove its veracity at that time.

In this particular PR, the context is slightly different.  `Hierarchy` is a plain JS class, and listener management is done by us (the application), not the browser's renderer.  As long as there is a path from the application's root to the `Hierarchy`, the `Hierarchy` will not be garbage collected, and neither will its listeners.  However, once the `Hierarchy` changes, due to loading a new graph, or expanding a grouped series node, we lose all references to it.  In this PR, the listeners on the Hierarchy are no longer accessible from the application's root, so they should be garbage collected as well.

I think the pattern in this PR boils down to something like https://gist.github.com/psybuzz/f368886b99b542156e5f7280cc9966b7
e.g. clients can add listeners to the hierarchy and never remove them, and once the hierarchy changes, the listeners will not be marked as collectable by the GC

> Yes. I believe all of our environment variables should be declared in a single location. I don't think we want env variable declarations scattered throughout the code. We should declare all configurables upfront in a single location where possible. This improves discoverability. It's true that means they are "further away" from where they're used, but their location is standardized, and only a single hop ("Jump to Definition") that colocates all of the environment variables.

While I get some of Ian's hesitation towards this, I think this would be more inline with how production ready configuration libraries generally work. 

It is nice being able to define the env vars at the site of use, but 
A) We should probably be adding some friction to adding new envvars, they aren't free and every axis of configuration is an axis to worry about testing and maintaining
B) Being able to inject the environment as a configuration object of all config rather than ad-hoc access to getenv would be a _massive_ unit testing win
C) Up front validation of configuration would be nice. I think there's some debate here about if for example an envvar is set invalid but then doesn't get used wouldn't it be nicer to ignore the error rather than telling the user to fix it, but I'm not sure that's an overall win.

@jtpereyda So, `default_signal_handler = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGWINCH)` saves the current SIGWINCH handler, `signal.signal(signal.SIGWINCH, default_signal_handler)` restores it. [Reference](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22916783/reset-python-sigint-to-default-signal-handler)
The position of the default_signal_handler saving  after ac4afff might be a little too far down in the code, moving it above `self._stdscr = curses.initscr()` would be safer, but it passed my tests as it is. 

This is necessary because curses sets up a custom handler. That handler allows interrupting the program at any point, messing with the timings and causing errors.
The default signal handler does literally nothing, so we just use that and check for a terminal size change periodically. Not the most responsive way, but it works.

The only thing that is strongly discouraged is a bare `any`, because it will shadow the builtin for that file and means that no where in that scope can we use `any`.

So, we should not do either of these:

```python
def any():
  pass

any = True
```

These things are okay though:

```python
platform = "any"  # quotes make it a string

ExpectedPlatformType.any  # attribute is safe for the caller (likely not for the original class)
```

So, this is all to say that yes, here we can use `any`, because the value is specified in the `enum` as a string so we never actually end up shadowing the builtin.

I wanted to turn this off because I'm reworking Toil to use logic that works for multiple nodes, which ends up actually keeping Directories' contents in Toil instead of on the filesystem. Right now in the implementation I have, the Directory contents are basically packed into strings that Toil passes around, meaning they can't be updated from elsewhere.

I could optimize it to check if the `single_machine` batch system is being used, or any batch system with a file job store, and then find a place for CWL to keep all its stuff in a local temp directory and support InplaceUpdateRequirement, but that seems awkward. I don't *think* the FileJobStore technically presents an interface to get a shared temp directory right now; I'm not really sure I understand how we were ensuring that files or Directories identified by paths weren't deleted when jobs ended before.

What I am hoping we might be able to do eventually is keep Directory contents in mutable files, which could be replaced from other jobs, allowing for side-effect updates from any machine to be visible on any other, with all the race conditions that you would expect in that scenario being exposed to the workflow. But I'm not sure that that semantically matches what `InplaceUpdateRequirement` is supposed to mean; Lon said it was added because people had big files that were too huge to copy, and allowing the side effects without actually being able to prevent the copy is no help to anyone.

> The concern is that if I'm on a linux distribution that sets that variable, I will cache miss on a distribution that doesn't set that variable...

Could you please elaborate on this concern? For example, in Pants it doesn't seem like this would be an issue. Our Ubuntu shards and Centos6 shards both don't set `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH`, so I don't see any immediate concern for Pants.

More generally, this is typically a distribution-set variable rather than individual. For example, Debian sets the value as the most recent value of its changelog. So, it does not seem likely that users within an org would have an issue of cache misses.

The reason to include support for `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` is to be a good citizen with this standard. I don't see why a user would want to use `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` over our hardcoded date, but Reproducible Builds makes a big hoopla about it so it seems like a best practice to respect.

Okay, onboard with getting the naming unified, but with the opposite preference. Here's why checkpoint makes more sense to me:
- `checkpoint` is already an ID. So we are comparing `checkpoint` to `changeset_id`
- there are other places (like tests) that the name is shortened to `changeset` (presumably because it's shorter/easier to type)
- Dropping from `changeset_id` to `changeset` dramatically changes the meaning. I expect a `changeset` to be something like a `DBDiff`, a set of all the changes (between two checkpoints).
- committing or discarding a changeset (ID) seems like we're only committing or discarding the changes that happened in that specific changeset
- committing a checkpoint is more intuitively committing everything that happened since the checkpoint.
- put another way: it's like the changeset for every single changeset ID is changing every time you add or delete to the journal (committing the first changeset has a different meaning after every touch of the journal)

Re: dropping `DB`, all of the classes that refer to the checkpoint (like `VMState`, `AccountDB`, etc) are using the `JournalDB` class and theoretically don't know/care that the `Journal` implementation exists. Of course I get that it's strange that `Journal` would reference `JournalDB`. I guess theoretically there should be two checkpoint classes, one that would only be used internally. That's kind of more babysitting of mypy than I feel like doing. I've come close to dropping `Journal` altogether and inlining it to `JournalDB` since we're not using `Journal` elsewhere and it's so performance sensitive. So I really didn't feel like wrangling mypy if that's where we're headed.

They kind of work opposite from each other as far as encoding/decoding is concerned, but they process callbacks in the same way.

Ugh, sorry, this actually doesn't work.  For consistency in the library, we actually _need_ the number of tokens here to match the number of tokens that we get out of the `embedder`.  This is so that we can create consistent labelings and have everything work.  Consider, for instance, that I want to label one of these tokens as the correct answer to a question.  I need to pass an index into this token list.  If that token list changes after I create my `Instance` (which happens when you add things in `tokens_to_indices`), then my label is going to be wrong.  I'll have to know about the change, and have code somewhere that changes my label to the right value, meaning that some arbitrary other `Field` has to know about the way that you've done this indexing.  Not good.

Our original pretrained-bert token indexer / embedder handled this by keeping a mapping back to the original tokens, and getting token vectors out again in the embedder.  If we want to add tokens in the `TokenIndexer`, we need to remove them in the `TokenEmbedder` for things to be consistent.  Otherwise things start to fall apart, and the abstractions don't actually work.  And the point of the `pretrained_transformer` indexer and embedder was that we weren't going to do any funny business with the tokens in the embedder, so that means we need to have all of the tokens we need come out of the `tokenizer`.  We _need_ this particular check to stay as it is.  I'm afraid that means we'll have to duplicate some of the logic that's in `transformers`, but I don't know of another way around this.  Can you think of one? 

I just looked through the methods in `transformers`, and the important ones assume that you're adding ids, not strings, so we can't actually use them, unless we do some hacky things inside of `tokens_to_indices` (which maybe we can do...?).

No, you still call the `filter()` function if there is an `_x_count` it just takes the `else` block of this function.

For filtering on `_x_count` the IR looks something like:
```
...  
Fold(...)
MarkLocation(...)
Unfold()
...
GlobalOperationsStart()
Filter(BinaryComposition(<operation>, FoldedContextField(..., field=_x_count),...)
...
```

vs a filter on a folded local field:

```
...  
Fold(...)
Filter(BinaryComposition(<operation>, LocalField(<local field name>, ...), ...)
MarkLocation(...)
Unfold()
...
```

If we're filtering on a local field, that happens between a `Fold` and `Unfold` block. This is added to the `SQLFoldObject` and added as a `WHERE` clause in the folded subquery (taking the `if self._current_fold is not None` block).

If we're filtering on `_x_count`, this happens after the `GlobalOperationsStart` block and is treated as a global filter, applied to the global `WHERE` clause (taking the `else` block).
This is necessary for now given the discussion [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/1909#discussion_r260850621)

For `grrrr` and other `no-sudo` commands, we stay with normal `self.py3.command_output`.

Quick preface -- I'm hoping this comment is helpful, from someone who's working to get trio into an internal app we use at work (but nonetheless in prod there, which is pretty neat). But I know there's work been done here and there's a whole trope about people coming into open source projects with demands and just yuck I'm really not trying to do that!

That being said, one thing I've found *extremely* useful is to, instead of passing a `capture_stdout` etc, is to pass a `stdout_redirect`, something like this:

```python
async def subproc_run(command, *, stdin_redirect=None, stdout_redirect=None,
                      stderr_redirect=None, **options):
    '''Any of stdin_redirect, stdout_redirect, and stderr_redirect
    should be trio channels. We'll read (stdin) or write (stdout/stderr)
    to those channels as needed.
    '''
```

Originally I started with the ``subprocess.run()`` replica [in the docs](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-io.html#running-a-process-and-waiting-for-it-to-finish), but one of the ways I'm using this is to call into terraform, and those commands can sometimes take on the order of a minute to run. So I'd be sitting there looking at my console for, like, a long time, wondering what was up, and then finally at the end it would dump the whole stdout into the terminal, by looking into the returned ``subprocess.CompletedProcess.stdout`` (or stderr), which was suboptimal.

So instead I retooled it a bit to accept trio channels instead. I kinda punted on the "what's an appropriate buffer size here" question, and this is still in-process code (also, there's some stuff I'm doing with an ``async with capture_output()`` helper object that's probably too cutesy-clever for something like trio)  -- but anyways, I put the implementation and a few example usages into [this gist](https://gist.github.com/Badg/d188f2c0c0bb684a1a08920bdac0d3eb).

Well, I saw two options for this:
1.` delete_age` get set each time the student starts or resumes their lab. It will look for the XBlock attribute value if that is `None`, use the global settings value. And with this approach, if the value is set in the admin it will simply be overridden when the student resumes, so having the option to edit it would be just pointless in this case. But this gives an option to edit the `delete_age` even mid-course for all students. The `delete_by` value can be set in the admin to optionally just throw away labs even before students resume (thinking that this would be a rare case).

2. `delete_age` gets set once when the student first launches the stack. It will look for the XBlock attribute value if that is `None`, use the global settings value. When the student resumes, if `delete_age` has a value, don't change it. So if we went and edited the value in the admin, the value will now persist. But we cannot make the change later via the XBlock attribute that would apply for all. If we want to change the delete_age for a single stack, we can do this here but if we would want to change it for all students, I am not sure how easy/comfortable would be doing that in the admin.

So I went with the first option and made the `delete_age` read-only, simply because the change would not take affect.

Consider an existing resource:

```c#
public class MyResource : CustomResource
{
    public MyResource()
        : base("type", "name", ResourceArgs.Empty)
    {
    }
}
```

If I left `= null` here, the above code would now see a new compiler error:

> The call is ambiguous between the following methods or properties: 'CustomResource.CustomResource(string, string, ResourceArgs?, CustomResourceOptions?)' and 'CustomResource.CustomResource(string, string, ResourceArgs?, CustomResourceOptions?, bool)'

The approach I took when adding the new optional parameters is from Jon Skeet ("Library version 1.1c"): https://codeblog.jonskeet.uk/2018/04/13/backward-compatibility-and-overloading/

> ## Optional parameters
>
> Optional parameters bring their own kind of pain. Suppose we have one optional parameter, but wish to add a second. We have three options, shown as 1.1a, 1.1b and 1.1c below.
>
> ```c#
> // Library version 1.0
> public void Foo(string x = "")
> 
> // Library version 1.1a
> // Keep the existing method, but add another one with two optional parameters.
> public void Foo(string x = "")
> public void Foo(string x = "", string y = "")
> 
> // Library version 1.1b
> // Just add the parameter to the existing method.
> public void Foo(string x = "", string y = "")
> 
> // Library version 1.1c
> // Keep the old method but make the parameter required, and add a new method
> // with both parameters optional.
> public void Foo(string x)
> public void Foo(string x = "", string y = "")
> ```
>
> Let’s think about a client that makes two calls:
>
> ```c#
> // Client
> static void Method()
> {
>     var library = new Library();
>     library.Foo();
>     library.Foo("xyz");
> }
> ```
>
> Library 1.1a keeps binary compatiblity, but breaks source compatibility: the `library.Foo()` is now ambiguous. The C# overloading rules prefer a method that doesn’t need the compiler to “fill in” any optional parameters, but it doesn’t have any preference in terms of _how many_ optional parameters are filled in.
>
> Library 1.1b keeps source compatibility, but breaks binary compatibility. Existing compiled code will expect to call a method with a single parameter – and that method no longer exists.
>
> Library 1.1c keeps binary compatibility, but is potentially odd around source compatibility. The `library.Foo()` call now resolves to the two-parameter method, whereas `library.Foo("xyz")` resolves to the one-parameter method (which the compiler prefers over the two-parameter method because it doesn’t need to fill in any optional parameters). Allocator always used two words of overhead for bytearrays. This was because in certain situations in the calling convention, bytearrays were interpreted in-place and so they needed an extra word allocated for the offset word. (The ABI encoding for a bytestring is basically `0x20 <offset in the dynamic section> | <1 length word> | <bytestring data>`). In this PR, there is no implicit interpretation of bytearrays in this way, and so the allocator does not need to allocate the extra word anymore.

OK we decided on:

1. Per-authenticator "hints" if at all possible. 
  a) draft some copy
   b) try adapt `IAuthenticator`.
2. `--debug-challenges` is tricky because it doesn't work for the `renew` verb and changing that semantic was deemed too risky.

Here's my attempt at writing the hints for (1). I think it's pretty neat because we can make it responsive to whatever is in `IConfig` at the time of execution.  Potentially we could also make it responsive to the error code in `achalls`, but interpreting ACME problems is maybe not a good idea because IME even Boulder makes some questionable error code choices sometimes.

Some questions/comments are inline.

`--webroot`
-------
Hint: the Certificate Authority tried to download the the challenge files created by the --webroot plugin, but reported failure. Ensure that the provided --webroot-path/-w is correct for the above domains and that files created there are accessible from the internet.

- It would be nice to print a path directly here but `[webroot_map]` makes that kind of tricky. Keeping in mind, this hint is per-order and not per-challenge.


`--dns-*`
-------
Hint: the Certificate Authority tried to verify the DNS TXT records created by the --{} plugin, but reported failure. Ensure that the above domains are managed by that DNS provider, or try increasing --{}-propagation-seconds.


`--apache`
-------
Hint: the Certificate Authority tried to verify the temporary Apache configuration changes made by the --apache plugin, but reported failure. Ensure that the above domains point to this Apache server. 


`--nginx`
-------
Hint: the Certificate Authority tried to verify the temporary nginx configuration changes made by the --nginx plugin, but reported failure. Ensure that the above domains point to this nginx server, or try increase --nginx-sleep-seconds. 

- Do we want to document outstanding authenticator erratum/bugs in these hints? For example, --apache still has problems with duplicate ServerNames sometimes. --nginx is still broken if you try to use it when Cloudflare is being used in Full/Strict mode.


`--manual` (with hooks)
-------
Hint: the Certificate Authority tried to verify the changes made by the --manual-auth-hook, but reported failure. Ensure that this hook is functioning correctly{ and that it waits a sufficient duration of time after creating a DNS TXT record}. Refer to {certbot} --help manual.


`--manual` (no hook)
-------
Hint: the Certificate Authority tried to {verify/download} the manually created {DNS TXT records/challenge files}, but reported failure. Ensure that the {DNS TXT records/challenge files} were created in the correct location{ or try waiting longer for DNS propagation on the next attempt}.

- Possibly we want to add a hint in the "--manual pause notification" to wait for DNS propagation, instead of describing it later.

```python
# Since python 3.7
context = ssl.create_default_context(ssl.Purpose.SERVER_AUTH)
# Reset to default OpenSSL values
context.check_hostname = False
context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
```

...was replaced with this one:

```python
# Since python 3.7

```

The `create_default_context()` code from Python 3.10.2:

```python
def create_default_context(purpose=Purpose.SERVER_AUTH, *, cafile=None,
                           capath=None, cadata=None):
    """Create a SSLContext object with default settings.

    NOTE: The protocol and settings may change anytime without prior
          deprecation. The values represent a fair balance between maximum
          compatibility and security.
    """
    if not isinstance(purpose, _ASN1Object):
        raise TypeError(purpose)

    # SSLContext sets OP_NO_SSLv2, OP_NO_SSLv3, OP_NO_COMPRESSION,
    # OP_CIPHER_SERVER_PREFERENCE, OP_SINGLE_DH_USE and OP_SINGLE_ECDH_USE
    # by default.
    if purpose == Purpose.SERVER_AUTH:
        # verify certs and host name in client mode
        context = SSLContext(PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT)
        context.verify_mode = CERT_REQUIRED
        context.check_hostname = True 
    elif purpose == Purpose.CLIENT_AUTH:
        context = SSLContext(PROTOCOL_TLS_SERVER)
    else:
        raise ValueError(purpose)

    if cafile or capath or cadata:
        context.load_verify_locations(cafile, capath, cadata)
    elif context.verify_mode != CERT_NONE:
        # no explicit cafile, capath or cadata but the verify mode is
        # CERT_OPTIONAL or CERT_REQUIRED. Let's try to load default system
        # root CA certificates for the given purpose. This may fail silently.
        context.load_default_certs(purpose)
    # OpenSSL 1.1.1 keylog file
    if hasattr(context, 'keylog_filename'):
        keylogfile = os.environ.get('SSLKEYLOGFILE')
        if keylogfile and not sys.flags.ignore_environment:
            context.keylog_filename = keylogfile
    return context
```

I see the following differences in the behaviour:

1. No `context.load_default_certs()` call. It is the goal of the change.
2. `PROTOCOL_TLS` instead of `PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT`.

The [PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT) differs this way:

> The protocol enables `CERT_REQUIRED` and `check_hostname` by default.

(And `PROTOCOL_TLS` is deprecated since Python 3.10.)

So all changes looks expected.

We can leave it as is or change a bit to avoid the deprecation warning (it is issued from `_ssl__SSLContext_impl()`):

```python
# Since python 3.7
context = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT)
# Reset to default OpenSSL values
context.check_hostname = False
context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
```

To be honest, I'm in a doubt that accepting expired / another host certificate is the good default behaviour. OTOH, it looks incorrect to require providing a CA certificate from a client. What would be the ideal default in my opinion:

1. Check hostname in the server's certificate against provided `host`.
2. Check server's certificate expiration.
3. Don't validate server's certificate if `ssl_ca_file` is not passed.

However I'm not sure that we can achieve this behaviour using the `ssl` module. So, maybe, current behaviour is the best possible one.

The `response` attribute of `Response` is the underlying container for `data`. `response` is always an iterable of bytes (or strings that are later encoded), whereas `data` is a dynamic property that is the rendered output of `response`.

that means the following things:
* `response.response` can never be None. For an empty response, it will be an empty list. `response.response is not None` will therefore always return true
* in the case that `response.response` contains a generator (in the kinesis streaming case for example) calling `response.data` will block until the entire generator has been consumed, so calling `if request.data` is in general not safe.

for setting data, it means the following:
`data` has getters and setters which operate on strings and bytes. calling `response.data = "foobar"` is the same as calling `response.set_data("foobar")`, which will set `self.response = ["foobar".encode('utf-8')]` (simplified). and also update the Content-Length headers appropriately. so whenever we know that something is a string or bytes, we can safely set `response.data = ...`, whereas if we're not sure whether something may be an iterator, it's better to use `response.set_response(...)` because that will do that check for us (at the cost of doing the check), which will again fall back to `response.data = ...` if the input is a string or bytes.

> Could there be a case here where response.response is falsy, but response.data has already been set (and should be returned)?

not that i can think of. if you set `response.data = b""` (to indicate a deliberately empty response), then `response.response` will be `[b""]` which will be truthy (which is btw exactly the case we are guarding against).
unless you set `response.response` to something very weird that overwrites `__bool__` then yes i guess you could run into that problem. but as you said that doesn't seem very likely to me.

so in summary:
* `response.response` may be set to an iterator -> `response.response` will be truthy
* we cant do `if response.data` checks because it would potentially consume the underlying chunked response stream
* an empty response (`data = ""` which is equivalent to `set_response("")`) -> `response` will be automatically truthy
* `data` never set -> `response` will be falsy

It's interesting that `._peek()` has now become the common pipeline execution path, which are slightly odd semantics. 🤔 I'm wondering if it would be cleaner to isolate optimization + execution a bit, maybe something like this:
```python
def _execute(self) -> Iterator[Dataset[T]]:
    self._optimize_stages()
    return PipelineExecutor(self)

def _peek(self) -> Dataset[T]:
    if self._first_dataset is None:
        self._dataset_iter = self._execute()
        self._first_dataset = next(self._dataset_iter)
    return self._first_dataset

def iter_datasets(self) -> Iterator[Dataset[T]]:
    # ...
    if self._dataset_iter is None:
        # No caching from peek, so we directly trigger execution.
        iter = self._execute()
    else:
        # Use cached dataset and iterator from past peek, and unlink the cached attrs
        # to more eagerly release memory.
        iter = itertools.chain([self._first_dataset], self._dataset_iter)
        self._dataset_iter = None
        self._first_dataset = None
```

Memory cost of a typical Service Instance Entry:
- **fullname**: around 40 bytes for `my-service._ipps._tcp.default.service.arpa`, and 255 bytes for maximum.
- **metadata**: around 40 bytes (`weight`, `priority`, `port` and pointers).
- **TXT data**: this value can vary from 1 byte to 65535 bytes. For typical real use cases, I think it should be around 30 ~ 200 bytes.
- Total size: 40 + 40 + 30 = 110 bytes.

Memory cost of a typical Host Instance Entry:
- **fullname**: around 30 bytes for `my-host.default.service.arpa`, and 255 bytes for maximum.
- **metadata**: around 30 bytes (`lease`, `key lease` and pointers).
- **address**: we use static array (`kMaxAddressesNum = 8`) for address list and it costs `8 * 16` bytes. I think for most cases, there will be a single address. I will change this value to `2` to allow possible 1.2 addresses (e.g. DUA).
- **ECDSA KEY**: 64 bytes.
- Total size: 30 + 30 + 32 + 64 156 bytes.


I remember that the duckhorn BR requires the SRP server to serve around 500 services from 250 clients (2 services for each client). This will definitely exceeds the maximum size of the internal heap. We can do some optimizations to the SRP server implementation to reduce the memory consumption for each service and host entry. For example, we can stores only the service instance name and service name but not the full name for each service entry. But I don't think the maximum size can be limited to the internal heap capability. I think for posix and other resourceful platforms, we should just use external heap for the Border Router.

I am not sure of the intention behind this change:

- We are basically calling `PushPull()` 5 times in row each time adding 1msec delay.
- This is then adding 5msec delay in main run loop of OT stack for every SPI transaction which is not good.
- Once we call `PushPull()`, if we have any pending tx frame we sent to peripheral and if it indicates that it accepted it, we clear tx frame. 
- Then the follow up 4 calls of `PushPull()` will simply send empty transactions.
- If the peripheral could not accept the frame, then we would anyway retry the same tx frame in next time we get to `Process()` and do `PushPull()`.

The primary thing we are worried about isn't speed, it's ABI safety.

The question is what function_record's should pybind11 try to manipulate?

Options are:
1. function_records only from the same .so (pointer comparison using local_internals)
2. function_records only from the same PYBIND11_INTERNALS_ID (pointer comparison using internals)
3. all function_records (std::strcmp using manual string)

See my response to the above discussion for what "manipulation" pybind11 does (but note that the exact type of manipulation probably doesn't matter that much for ABI; any use is UB and a bad idea).

Shallow copies of proto messages are not possible. A shallow copy means constructing a new object and then populating it with references to the child objects found in the original. Let's say you have a message with in a message and now if you shallow copy you get the new object of the outer message, but for the inner message you share the reference to that in the original. This has so many complications and is not used for proto messages.

This is why deep copy is preferred and used.

The header files located at the root of `/ops/` folder contain the "public" methods for C++ API while the cpp files contain the code necessary for calling the dispatcher and registering the operators. They are not meant to contain code of the actual implementation of the method.

Up until now, there was very little need to share code between the CPU and CUDA implementations and as a result you won't find in torchvision's codebase an example that does what you want to do. One approach is to follow the pattern used at PyTorch and move the common code is separate files in the `/ops/cpu/` folder. See the [Loops.h](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c371542efc31b1abfe6f388042aa3ab0cef935f2/aten/src/ATen/native/cpu/Loops.h) file which is used both by [CPU kernels](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c371542efc31b1abfe6f388042aa3ab0cef935f2/aten/src/ATen/native/cpu/ComplexKernel.cpp) and [quantized](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c371542efc31b1abfe6f388042aa3ab0cef935f2/aten/src/ATen/native/quantized/cpu/qpool.cpp#L7).
